title: pi-context-cli id-flag naming diverges across id-taking ops (--id vs --itemId vs --taskId vs --parentId vs --unitId), with no normalized convention
description: The reflecting pi-context-cli derives each op's CLI flag names verbatim from that op's typebox parameters-schema property keys (packages/pi-context-cli/src/cli.ts parseOpArgs reads props[field] off objectSchema(op).properties; an unknown key throws 'unknown flag'). Because the op-registry authored those property names ad hoc per op, the single-item-id argument is spelled differently across ops with no shared convention: read-block-item and resolve-item-by-id use --id; find-references and walk-ancestors use --itemId; context-walk-descendants uses --parentId; complete-task uses --taskId; gather-execution-context uses --unitId; promote-item uses --source. update-block-item / remove-block-item / nested-item ops select the id inside a --match JSON predicate; relation ops use --parent / --child. Driving the CLI, the natural guess --id fails on find-references with 'unknown flag: --id' (exit 2), forcing a re-read of --help to discover --itemId. The divergence is purely a property-name authoring inconsistency in the op-registry surfaced 1:1 by the flag-derivation, not a behavioral defect: each op works once the right flag is supplied. Impact is dogfooding friction (wrong-flag guesses, failed invocations, help round-trips) rather than data correctness.

evidence.reference: resolve-item-by-id parameters declare 'id: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --id
read-block-item parameters declare 'id: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --id
walk-ancestors parameters declare 'itemId: Type.String' — same single-item-id concept spelled --itemId, diverging from --id
find-references parameters declare 'itemId: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --itemId; calling with --id throws 'unknown flag: --id' (exit 2), the trigger of this gap
context-walk-descendants parameters declare 'parentId: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --parentId
complete-task parameters declare 'taskId: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --taskId
gather-execution-context parameters declare 'unitId: Type.String' — id-flag spelled --unitId

parseOpArgs derives flag names 1:1 from objectSchema(op).properties keys (props[field]); a token whose stripped name is absent from properties throws UsageError('unknown flag: ' + tok). The CLI has no id-flag aliasing/normalization layer, so each op's schema property-name spelling is the only accepted flag for that op's item-id argument.

impact: Usability/dogfooding footgun, not a correctness defect. Each id-taking op works once the correct flag is supplied; the cost is wrong-flag guesses and failed invocations (exit 2) when driving the CLI directly. The most common single-item-id spelling is --id (read-block-item, resolve-item-by-id), so --id is the natural guess and the ops that diverge from it (find-references, walk-ancestors as --itemId; context-walk-descendants as --parentId) reliably mis-fire. Because the CLI auto-reflects op-registry property names, every future id-taking op inherits whatever spelling its author chose, so the divergence grows unless a convention is established.
proposed_resolution: Establish a single canonical convention for the single-item-id argument across id-taking ops; the mechanism is to be chosen canonically. Options: (a) normalize the op-registry property names so every single-item-id parameter is the same key (rename --itemId/--parentId/--unitId/--taskId to a shared spelling at the schema source, accepting that this changes the op input-schema property name as seen by both the Pi tool surface and the CLI); (b) add an id-flag alias layer in pi-context-cli parseOpArgs (packages/pi-context-cli/src/cli.ts) that accepts a canonical --id alongside each op's native id-property flag, leaving op-registry schemas untouched (CLI-only ergonomic, no Pi-tool surface change); or (c) introduce a per-op-registry-entry declarative alias map the CLI consumes. Whichever is chosen, also assess relation/match-predicate ops (--parent/--child, --match) for whether a consistent id-selection idiom is warranted. Add a parity/lint check that flags new id-taking ops whose item-id property deviates from the convention.
